zotero.abstractNote: Object-centric representation learning aims to decompose complex scenes into sets of entities. While many heuristic approaches have been proposed, a theoretical understanding of when and why these models succeed remains elusive. In this work, we provide a formal framework for object-centric representation learning based on the principle of independent causal mechanisms. We prove that under certain conditions, object-centric representations are identifiable from observational data. We further show that these conditions are satisfied by a broad class of generative models, including those based on slot-based architectures. Finally, we demonstrate that our theoretical results provide a principled explanation for the success of existing object-centric models and suggest new directions for future research.
